title: Jack TEF set for FW14B drive at Goodwood while F1 is out of town
meta: Published: 26 March 2026 | By Yuki Tanaka, Heritage & History Writer | F1CC News
caption: Goodwood Festival Showcase.
body:
Jack TEF announced he will have the opportunity to drive Nigel Mansell's iconic FW14B Formula 1 car at the Goodwood Festival next weekend. He noted, with the wry smile of a man who has spent his entire adult life inside the paddock, that this happens while “the real Formula 1 is out of town.” The invitation extends to fans: Williams HQ will be stationed in the Goodwood Paddock, where Eren Aygen, Lucas Koopman, Hamish Porter, and Jack himself will be present to meet the faithful. The announcement was met with tremendous excitement online, a reminder that even in the midst of a gruelling championship campaign, the pull of history is irresistible.

The Williams FW14B is not merely a racing car. It is a landmark in the technological evolution of the sport, a machine so far ahead of its time that its influence still echoes through the engineering philosophies of the modern grid. Designed by Adrian Newey and Patrick Head, the car featured active suspension and traction control systems that were, in 1992, the closest Formula 1 had come to genuine artificial intelligence on wheels. The suspension's hydraulic actuators could read the road surface and adjust ride height and damping in real time, keeping the car perfectly flat through corners and allowing Mansell and Riccardo Patrese to carry speeds that left their rivals gasping. In the spirit of '92, when the FW14B won ten of sixteen races and delivered Mansell his long‑awaited world championship, the car remains a touchstone for what is possible when engineering ambition runs without restraint.

Goodwood itself is no ordinary venue. The Festival of Speed, founded by the Earl of March in 1993, has grown from a private gathering of enthusiasts into the world's most celebrated shrine to motor racing history. Its famous hill climb, a ribbon of tarmac winding through the Sussex countryside, has been graced by everything from Edwardian land‑speed record cars to modern Formula 1 machinery. The sight of a championship‑winning Williams, still bearing the red five of Mansell's number, roaring past the flint walls and ancient trees of the Goodwood Estate, will be a moment of communion between the sport's golden age and its present. “Goodwood is where the ghosts of our past get to stretch their legs,” a period Autosport columnist wrote in 2005. “It is the only place where you can hear a V10 and a V12 on the same afternoon and believe, for a moment, that time has folded in on itself.”

The logistics of transporting the FW14B — along with its highly specialised support equipment and the veteran mechanics who still understand the complex active suspension system — is an immense undertaking. Williams Heritage has spent the last three months carefully rebuilding the hydraulic actuators and recalibrating the vintage electronics specifically for this run. The active suspension system relies on a web of sensors and servo‑controlled valves that were state‑of‑the‑art three decades ago but today require the kind of arcane knowledge that only a handful of retired engineers possess. One of those engineers, now in his seventies, was coaxed out of retirement for this project; he spent two weeks in the Grove workshop, surrounded by wiring diagrams printed on fading thermal paper, muttering to himself as he coaxed life back into the system. The sheer historical importance of keeping this iconic chassis running cannot be casually overstated: it represents the absolute technological zenith of F1's most unrestricted era, a period before cost caps and standardised parts, when a team with a visionary designer and a fearless budget could reshape the competitive order in a single winter.

For Jack TEF, the pressure is surprisingly high despite the exhibition nature of the event. His own career — 201 points and third in the 2025 championship, a transition from cockpit to team principal completed in a single off‑season — is a story of speed and sacrifice. He knows, perhaps better than anyone, that a racing driver's relationship with a car is built on trust. The FW14B demands a different kind of trust. “You don't just 'drive' the FW14B,” he noted during a simulator session, his voice carrying the reverence of a man who has studied Mansell's onboards frame by frame. “You have to trust the active suspension to catch the car, which goes against every natural instinct you have as a driver. The car does things a modern chassis simply cannot do — it corners flat, it rides the kerbs like they're not there, it asks you to believe in systems you can't see. It's a wildly fascinating piece of history.”

With Eren Aygen and Lucas Koopman observing from the sidelines — their own 2026 Williams machines parked, momentarily, in the shadow of their ancestor — the entire Williams family will be thoroughly united to celebrate their iconic heritage while simultaneously gathering energy for the gruelling remainder of their ongoing campaign. The Williams team that Jack TEF now leads is a far cry from the dominant force that Mansell and Patrese propelled to a constructors' title in 1992. The modern Williams fights for points, not championships. Yet the FW14B is a reminder that the same walls in Grove once housed the most feared engineering department in the sport. For Aygen and Koopman, who were not yet born when Mansell lifted his title, the chance to watch their team principal wrestle a piece of their own legacy up the hill will be a lesson no debrief can teach.

This Goodwood showcase offers a rare chance to bridge the golden era of the 1990s with the modern grid, and fans are expected to flood the paddocks just to hear the legendary roar of that engine echoing up the hill. The Renault V10, an engine that screamed at over 14,000 rpm, produces a sound that no hybrid power unit can replicate — a banshee wail that cuts through the Sussex air and lodges itself in the memory of everyone who hears it. Among the crowd will be Mansell himself, now a grandfather, who has been known to shed a quiet tear when he hears his old car run. The FW14B carried him to the championship he had chased for a decade, and the bond between driver and machine is something that decades cannot erode.

Thirty‑four years have passed since Mansell planted the FW14B on pole at Silverstone and heard the roar of a home crowd that knew they were witnessing history. That car now sits in a tent at Goodwood, polished and primed, waiting for a young team principal who was not alive when its engine first fired. The time between then and now stretches like a long shadow across the paddock. When Jack TEF accelerates away from the start line and the V10 screams into the Sussex morning, the 1992 championship will not be a memory. It will be alive.
